title: 24 Meskerem — The Arrival in Egypt & The Assembly
saints: The Arrival of Christ in Egypt (Monthly Commemoration)
St. Thekla the Protomartyr of Women, Equal-to-the-Apostles
St. Silvanus of Gaza and Companions, Martyrs
narrative:
The twenty-fourth day of Meskerem holds the monthly commemoration of the Arrival of Christ in Egypt — that sacred journey of the Holy Family which fills so much of the Coptic and Ethiopian sacred geography with the fragrance of the divine presence. This second appearance of the Egyptian theme in the same month as the first (celebrated on the twentieth) is not redundant but cumulative: the first commemorated the departure from Bethlehem, the flight from Herod; this one commemorates the arrival, the settling-in, the moment when the refugee family found shelter in the ancient land of bondage that had become, for them, a land of refuge. Egypt receives Christ as Egypt received the Patriarch Joseph — as a place that seems hostile from the outside but becomes, in the providence of God, the very place where the purposes of heaven are preserved and nurtured through the season of danger.

St. Thekla the Protomartyr of Women and Equal-to-the-Apostles is one of the most beloved and widely venerated women in the entire Eastern Christian tradition, and her feast on the twenty-fourth of Meskerem is among the great women's feasts of the year. She heard Paul preaching in Iconium, was transformed by the word she received, broke her engagement, and embarked on a missionary career of extraordinary breadth and courage — surviving multiple attempts to execute her, baptizing herself in extremity when water was available and no minister was present, and dying peacefully in extreme old age at Seleucia after decades of healing and teaching ministry. The early Church's regard for Thekla was such that she was venerated as virtually an apostle, and in some communities her memory was celebrated alongside Paul's as a co-worker in the apostolic mission rather than merely a disciple.

St. Silvanus of Gaza and his companions were Palestinian monastics who were seized from their communities and martyred during the early fourth century persecution. Silvanus was a bishop who had spent years as a monk before his consecration, and his death alongside his monastic brothers reflects the peculiar vulnerability of the Palestinian monastic communities of this era: communities too visible to be ignored, too faithful to be compromised, and too embedded in their particular landscape to be easily evacuated. His martyrdom at Gaza is a reminder that the Land which the Holy Family traversed on their flight to Egypt — the Land through which Philip ran to meet the Ethiopian eunuch — was itself a land of martyrs, a ground saturated with the blood of those who kept faith in the crossroads of empires.

The Arrival in Egypt, Thekla, and Silvanus together on the twenty-fourth day present the community with a theology of faithful arrival. The Holy Family arrived in Egypt as refugees and found it a place of divine preservation. Thekla arrived at the apostolic mission as an outsider to its official structures and found it a place of divine commissioning. Silvanus arrived at his final martyrdom through the long preparation of the monastic life and found it a place of completion. Every arrival the community makes — at the new year, at the post-Maskal month, at the daily prayer, at the year's final days — is an arrival in the same divine territory: the space where God receives those who come, and preserves what He receives.
